name: Bistro Beppinsha (カリー＆ワイン ビストロべっぴん舎 お茶の水本店)
cuisine: Medicinal (薬膳) Japanese-style spice curry & wine bistro
reasoning: GF: The ONLY basis for a GF label is the shop's own marketing phrasing 「小麦粉を使わないグルテンフリーカリー」/「小麦粉を使わないさらさらの薬膳カレー」, echoed uncritically by food/tourism blogs (rurubu, tokyo-tabearuki, kanda-curry). This is a single-origin claim with ZERO independent celiac-grade corroboration — no FindMeGlutenFree/HappyCow celiac reviews exist. Critically, Japanese "小麦粉不使用/no wheat flour" is the classic celiac trap: it means "no roux," NOT gluten-free. Wheat-containing soy sauce, Worcester-type sauce, and miso contain no wheat "flour" but are pervasive hidden seasonings in Japanese curry broths and tare, and nothing in any source confirms they are absent from Beppinsha's wine-and-spice broth (香味野菜スープ). The shop shows no celiac/allergen protocol and is a shared 14-seat curry+wine bistro, so its グルテンフリー is almost certainly a low-flour marketing term, not a celiac claim. The documented lunch set also bundles potato salad (mayo/egg) and pickles of unverified composition. Under the evidence bar, a single loose-marketing source in a soy-sauce-heavy cuisine with no protocol and unconfirmed hidden wheat cannot support "options" for a celiac child — a false "safe" here risks the ER. Downgrade to "ask": the flour-free design is a genuine starting point, but the diner must confirm directly that no wheat soy/Worcester is used in the broth, tare, or sides and ask about cross-contact. VEGAN: unchanged at "ask" and survives — core curries contain chicken drumstick, beef tendon, and shrimp; the set includes mayo-based potato salad (egg); the "vegetable soup" base may contain animal stock; no advertised vegan curry, though a mostly-vegetable version might be negotiable. No hidden-dashi disconfirmation found, but no vegan confirmation either. I could not disprove closure of the main Ochanomizu store (it appears active); a separate Jimbocho branch is marked Closed on Tabelog — not the same location.
red_flags: GF rests entirely on the shop's own marketing term (グルテンフリー/小麦粉を使わない) with no independent celiac-grade corroboration — a single source can never exceed 'options' and here does not even reach it safely
Japanese '小麦粉不使用' (no wheat flour) is not gluten-free: wheat-containing soy sauce / Worcester / miso are common hidden curry seasonings and are NOT confirmed absent from the wine-spice broth or tare
No celiac/allergen protocol; shared 14-seat curry+wine kitchen — cross-contact plausible and unmanaged
Lunch set bundles potato salad (mayo/egg) and pickles of unverified ingredients — both wheat and animal content unknown
Official site, Tabelog, and every review blog returned HTTP 403 to automated fetch — current menu/ingredient text could not be independently verified
A separate Beppinsha Jimbocho branch is listed as Closed on Tabelog; verify the Ochanomizu main store is still operating and menu unchanged

disconfirming_evidence.text: Japanese gluten-free authority explains most soy sauce contains wheat and is not gluten-free; only tamari/isiru substitutes are safe — i.e. a Japanese dish can be '小麦粉不使用' yet still contain wheat via soy sauce, so Beppinsha's flour-free claim does not establish celiac safety.

The documented lunch set includes rice, potato salad, pickled onions and coffee — the potato salad is mayonnaise/egg based and neither its nor the pickles' wheat/allergen content is disclosed, undercutting a blanket 'options' GF and any vegan reading.

name: BIOSSA (ビオッサ)
reasoning: I attacked the 'dedicated' GF label hard and it not only survived — it strengthened. BIOSSA clears BOTH prongs of the evidence bar for 'dedicated': (1) the shop's OWN explicit claim — they state 完全グルテンフリー, that they do not bring wheat into the production site (小麦を持ち込まない完全グルテンフリー製造工場), and they explicitly name 小麦アレルギー・セリアック病 as who it is safe for; (2) MULTIPLE independent celiac-grade corroborations, exceeding the >=1 requirement. The decisive corroboration is third-party GFCO (Gluten-Free Certification Organization) certification — an actual audited standard requiring <10ppm gluten — which multiple independent sources (Amazon official listing, Tokyo metropolitan TOKYO UPDATES, endlessdistances celiac guide, Tokyo Weekender) report BIOSSA holds, describing it as the first/only Japanese rice-flour-bread bakery to obtain GFCO. GFCO certification is stronger than the typical 'dedicated' basis (a shop's self-declaration + a review); it is a real audited gluten cap. FindMeGlutenFree lists it as a 'Dedicated Gluten-Free Bakery' with positive celiac reviews. I searched specifically for 'got glutened,' negative celiac reviews, wheat in ingredients, menu changes, or closure and found NONE — no disconfirming gluten evidence exists. There is no wheat-central-cuisine trap here: it is a 100% rice-flour bakery, so the wheat-cuisine downgrade rule does not apply. GF stays 'dedicated.' On vegan: 'options' is correct and I did not downgrade further — many items are genuinely plant-based (rice flour, 米油 rice oil, てんさい糖 beet sugar; the Amazon 7-item set is labeled Vegan; the 豆乳クリームパン is soy-based with no dairy/egg), but I confirmed by reading ingredient lists that specific items are NOT vegan — the 塩バターロール contains unsalted butter (declared dairy allergen). No hidden dashi/katsuo/egg/honey was found beyond the openly declared butter/dairy in specific loaves, which 'options' already captures. So vegan = 'options' (buyer must check the specific loaf). Net: keep both labels.
red_flags: Vegan/allergen (NOT gluten): BIOSSA's own product pages state the facility also processes products containing dairy, eggs, peanuts and shrimp, so cross-contact exists for those allergens — relevant to strict vegans and to non-gluten allergen sufferers, but NOT a wheat/gluten risk since no wheat enters the facility.

Specific non-vegan items confirmed by ingredient list: 塩バターロール (salt butter roll) contains unsalted butter (declared dairy). Some sweet breads may contain butter/egg — vegan shoppers must verify the individual loaf.

The 'dedicated' GF verdict rests substantially on GFCO certification reported via secondary sources (Amazon official listing, Tokyo metro article, celiac guides); I could not load the biossajapan.com About page or the GFCO registry directly (both returned 403 to the fetch tool), so Greg should confirm the GFCO certificate is current at the human gate.
Takeout-only shop ~1.2km from Suidobashi / ~9-10 min from Myogadani; closed Tuesdays — no on-site risk but confirm hours before a trip.

disconfirming_evidence.text: No disconfirming gluten evidence found: targeted searches for 'got glutened at BIOSSA', negative celiac reviews, wheat in ingredients, shared-equipment gluten exposure, menu changes, or closure returned nothing negative. FindMeGlutenFree reviews are positive and label it a Dedicated Gluten-Free Bakery.

Only genuine caveat (vegan/allergen, not gluten): BIOSSA product pages state the manufacturing facility also processes products containing dairy, eggs, peanuts and shrimp, creating cross-contact risk for those allergens.
Confirmed non-vegan item: 塩バターロール ingredient list includes unsalted butter (無塩バター), declared allergen: dairy — so the shop is not fully vegan and 'options' (not 'full') is the correct vegan status.

name: Cafe de Forte (カフェ・ド・フォルテ)
cuisine: Rice-flour (米粉) cafe — Japanese home-style light meals, pancakes & sweets
reasoning: GF: Downgrade from 'options' to 'ask'. The decisive disconfirmer is that this is NOT a dedicated GF kitchen — two independent search summaries of the shop's own materials confirm it runs a rice-flour (米粉) line AND a regular wheat-flour (小麦粉) line side by side in the same small kitchen ('米粉メニューだけでなく小麦粉を使った通常メニューも提供'), and the shop itself disclaims '全てのメニューがグルテンフリーではありません' and 'アレルギー完全対応ではございませんので、ご注意ください', asking allergy guests to confirm in advance. The independent JP guide (glutenfree.empacede.co.jp) does not corroborate safe handling — it WARNS to 'check with the shop whether a menu item contains wheat.' A Japanese Consumer Affairs Agency notice separately warns that rice-flour products themselves sometimes contain wheat/gluten ingredients and that allergy accidents have occurred. Per the evidence bar, 'options' implies reliably identifiable safe items; with active wheat-flour cooking in a shared kitchen, an explicit no-full-compliance disclaimer, and NO verified independent celiac-grade corroboration of dedicated prep, that reliability is not established. The single 'dedicated / celiac felt safe' hit could not be verified (FindMeGlutenFree 403; cited address 2-10-19 mismatches the official 2-14-9, indicating likely conflation) and is given no weight. For a celiac-first app, when in doubt, downgrade → 'ask'. Vegan: 'limited' survives and stays. Menu is dairy/meat-centric (Iwaizumi yogurt, butter, vanilla ice cream, roast beef, hamburg, beef stew, chicken); only a vegetable salad and plain rice-flour breads/noodles are plausibly adaptable, with hidden-animal-ingredient risk (butter in breads, possible dashi/bonito in noodle broths) and zero confirmed vegan dishes — 'limited' already tells vegans to ask and expect few options.

Explicit no-full-allergy-compliance disclaimer
No verified celiac corroboration
Unverifiable/conflated FMGF 'safe' claim
Rice-flour products can still contain wheat (CAA warning)
No confirmed vegan dish; hidden butter/dashi risk

disconfirming_evidence.text: 米粉メニューだけでなく小麦粉を使った通常メニューも提供 — the cafe offers regular menus using wheat flour alongside rice-flour menus, so wheat flour is actively handled in the same kitchen (cross-contamination risk).
アレルギー完全対応ではございませんので、ご注意ください — the shop explicitly states it does NOT provide complete allergy accommodation.
メニューに小麦成分が含まれているかどうかは、お店で確認してください — independent JP GF guide warns wheat may be present and to confirm with the shop rather than assume safety.

Unverifiable 'dedicated gluten free / I am celiac and felt safe' claim cited address 2-chōme-10-19 Kasuga, which does not match the official 2-14-9 小石川Jフラッツ; the FindMeGlutenFree page could not be fetched (403). Treated as unreliable/conflated, given no weight.

name: Be green by KIELO COFFEE (ビー グリーン バイ キエロ コーヒー)
cuisine: Vegan & gluten-free café, specialty coffee roastery and gluten-free bakery
reasoning: GF: Downgrade high -> options. The all-gluten-free, rice-flour (米粉) baking model is real and multi-sourced (japanese-heart, rurubu, afterthree, vegewel, note/CafeSnap blogs all state every sweet/baked good is vegan & GF), which is why this is not 'ask' or 'no'. But 'high' requires >=2 independent CREDIBLE sources of real GF/celiac HANDLING, and every source I found is a vegan/lifestyle directory or blog — none is celiac-grade, none describes an allergen protocol, and no celiac reviewer confirms a safe experience. Decisively, the one celiac-focused platform (FindMeGlutenFree / Atly) explicitly warns the KIELO brand is 'NOT a dedicated gluten-free facility and may not be safe for those with celiac disease,' has 'no specific gluten-free menu,' and says to contact directly. There is also brand ambiguity: the original 'KIELO COFFEE' (Akihabara/Taito) is a mixed café with only some GF sweets, so a celiac searching the brand name can land in an unsafe kitchen. Unaddressed gluten vectors (flavored syrups, chai, and Japanese cafés' common 麦茶/barley tea) are nowhere confirmed GF. Given the asymmetric cost (celiac child to the ER vs. a double-check), the correct conservative call is 'options': dependable GF-forward menu exists, but verify at point of sale — not celiac-'high'. Vegan: Keep 'full'. Fully plant-based is strongly and independently corroborated (all milks oat/almond/soy/pistachio with no dairy upcharge, all desserts vegan, Vegewel-listed, concept 'All for green'); no dashi/egg/honey/dairy vectors surfaced in any source, and as a sweets-and-coffee café there is no savory bonito/dashi exposure. Vegan survives adversarial review.
red_flags: No published celiac/allergen protocol; small shop
FindMeGlutenFree/Atly explicitly warns the KIELO brand is NOT a dedicated GF facility and 'may not be safe for those with celiac disease' with no specific GF menu — contact directly
Zero celiac-grade corroboration; no celiac reviewer confirms a safe (non-glutened) experience
Brand ambiguity: original KIELO COFFEE (Akihabara) is a mixed kitchen with only some GF sweets, not all-GF like the Be green location
Potential unverified gluten vectors not addressed by any source: flavored syrups, chai, and barley tea (麦茶) common in Japanese cafés
All GF/vegan evidence originates from lifestyle/vegan directories and blogs, not allergy-focused or celiac sources

No allergen or celiac cross-contact protocol is published on the shop's own channels (afterthree.jp/begreen, stores.jp site, Instagram); GF/vegan claims come only from lifestyle/vegan directories and blogs.

name: Sumatra Curry Kyoeido (スマトラカレー 共栄堂)
reasoning: The only basis for the GF label is the shop's own repeated statement that the dark sauce uses no wheat flour (「濃褐色のソースは小麦粉を一切使わず、20数種類の香辛料を1時間かけて炒めて作ります」). That is a taste/tradition marketing claim about the ROUX thickener, NOT an allergen or celiac claim — and it does not cover the rest of the dish. Critically, independent sources (kanda-curry / san-tatsu / mononcle-cited descriptions surfaced in search) confirm each meat is simmered in a SEPARATE bouillon base ("具材ごとにカレーソースのベースとなるブイヨンを変え、別々に煮込んでいる"). Commercial Japanese bouillon/consommé bases frequently contain wheat, and this vector is entirely unaddressed by the flour claim — a plausible hidden-gluten source. There is ZERO celiac-grade corroboration: no FindMeGlutenFree or HappyCow entries exist, and no review describes real GF handling. This is a 1924 single-item curry counter with a shared kitchen and no allergen protocol; the shop's site returns 403 and offers no allergen page. Under the evidence bar, a single (marketing) source cannot even support 'options' safely when it leaves the bouillon, seasonings, and cross-contact unverified. Presuming the label wrong until it survives, and given asymmetric harm to a celiac child, this must drop to 'ask' — a celiac has to directly confirm the per-meat bouillon and cross-contact before eating. The potage soup (confirmed a free service, likely flour-thickened) and fukujinzuke are correctly flagged as skip. VEGAN: 'no' survives fully — every curry is built on separately simmered pork/chicken/beef/shrimp/tongue with meat bouillon bases; there is no vegetable-only curry.

name: LA VORO Iidabashi (LA VORO 飯田橋店 / ラ ボーロ)
cuisine: Gluten-free rice-flour baumkuchen & yogashi (Western confectionery, takeout)
reasoning: GF: The cuisine is inherently low-risk for celiac — a takeout-only single-product confectionery whose ENTIRE lineup is 100% 生米粉 (raw rice flour from their own 常陸小田米). There is no wheat product, no tare/soy-sauce/broth/shared-fryer surface, and no savory menu, so the classic contamination vectors this protocol screens for do not apply. Multiple independent sources describe real GF handling: the ichigaya-keizai press piece, the shop's own statement (「自家製の生米粉を使用した…グルテンフリーのため安心してお召し上がりいただけます」), and several independent food blogs (kengaku-walk, chikudays, tsukuba-guruken, tsukubalive, entamemaimai) that repeatedly state the Tsukuba factory brings in no wheat flour at all (「工場には一切の小麦粉を入れず、完全なグルテンフリー」). That is well beyond the >=2-independent-source bar for 'high'. It falls short of 'dedicated' because (a) the wheat-free-WORKSHOP claim originates from third-party blogs rather than a primary shop statement I could read, and (b) I found no celiac-grade corroboration (no FindMeGlutenFree/HappyCow celiac testimonial). Adversarial searches for glutened reports, illness, allergic reactions, negative/まずい reviews, menu changes, or closure returned nothing. So 'high' survives; nothing pushes it down.\n\nVegan: Every product carries 卵・米粉・乳 (egg, rice flour, milk) and is built on eggs, cream and butter (モンコーネ adds 発酵バター + アーモンドパウダー). Nothing on the menu is remotely vegan. 'no' clearly survives.
red_flags: Could not read any primary source directly — the agent proxy returned HTTP 403 on the shop site (lavoro.base.shop/about), Tabelog review lists, and every food blog; all corroboration rests on WebSearch model-synthesized summaries, a reliability gap.

The 'wheat-free factory / no wheat brought in' claim that would justify 'dedicated' comes from third-party blogs, NOT a verified primary shop statement I could read; the shop's own confirmed wording phrases only the PRODUCTS (not the workshop) as gluten-free.
No celiac-grade corroboration found (no FindMeGlutenFree/HappyCow celiac review), so the label cannot rise to 'dedicated' despite the reassuring all-rice-flour lineup.
Shop opened April 2024 (Iidabashi is the 2nd branch); relatively new location with limited review history for a celiac to lean on.

disconfirming_evidence.text: No disconfirming evidence found: targeted adversarial searches for 体調不良/グルテン反応/アレルギー症状/まずい/口コミ悪い and for glutened/negative celiac reviews returned no complaints, no illness reports, and no cross-contamination reports.

name: 浅草 更科天狐（さらしな てんこ） (Asakusa Sarashina Tenko)
reasoning:
The current 'high' GF label does not survive adversarial scrutiny. The strongest independent celiac-grade source, FindMeGlutenFree, explicitly states this is NOT a dedicated gluten-free facility, describes it as only '99% gluten free' (not 100%), and reports that gluten-containing items are marked on the menu with a 🌾 emoji — i.e., wheat IS present on premises. A Japanese-language source corroborates that 'some menu items may contain wheat' (一部のメニューには小麦が含まれる場合がある). This directly contradicts the label's framing of an all-gluten-free operation. Additionally, the restaurant serves fried shrimp tempura, yet no source confirms a dedicated fryer or cross-contact protocol for fried items (the label itself already conceded this gap). One FMGF reviewer reported difficult English communication and no clear allergen labelling, recommending a Japanese celiac card for very sensitive diners. The chef's claim that gluten is 'prepared separately' is a shared-kitchen mitigation, not a dedicated-kitchen guarantee.

Positive celiac reviews DO exist (multiple celiacs report no symptoms; staff proactively warned an item was not celiac-safe), and the soba program is a genuine, credibly-handled GF accommodation — so the label is not 'no' or 'ask'. But for a celiac child in a shared kitchen where wheat is confirmed present, with unverified fryer/cross-contact protocols and no dedicated facility, 'high' overstates confidence. The correct conservative call is 'options': real GF menu items exist and are reasonably handled, but it is not a celiac-safe-by-default / dedicated environment. Per the ER-risk asymmetry and the mandate to downgrade in doubt, downgrade high→options.

Vegan stays 'ask' (already conservative). New positive evidence of pervasive animal ingredients: the signature 'Edo tsuyu' is bonito/katsuobushi-based dashi (confirmed by English media describing 'finest bonito essence'), plus duck-broth dishes, shrimp tempura, and chicken appear on the menu. No source confirms any vegan/plant-based dashi or menu. This confirms the dashi is fish-based (not vegan) but does not fully rule out that plain soba + vegetable items could be adapted, so 'ask' remains appropriate rather than a hard 'no'.

name: 悦納 (injoy) (Etsunō (Injoy))
reasoning: GF: The current 'ask' label survives the adversarial pass and is the correct conservative floor. Every source confirms a wheat-central shojin/oriental-vegan kitchen: signature dishes are dandan ramen / tan-tan men with HANDMADE (wheat) noodles, hot-and-sour noodles, and gyoza (wheat wrappers). Shojin ryori additionally relies on wheat gluten (fu/seitan) as a staple protein, so wheat is pervasive throughout the kitchen. No source — including the shop's own site and menu page — describes any dedicated GF protocol, dedicated fryer, GF menu labeling, or allergen handling. Japanese soy sauce/tare almost certainly contains wheat and the broth composition is undocumented, so cross-contamination risk is high. Per REVIEW_PROTOCOL, wheat-central cuisine with no explicit GF accommodation is 'no' or 'ask' and NEVER 'options'/'high'/'dedicated'. 'ask' (call ahead and confirm; naturally-GF rice items like bibimbap/chimaki may exist but must be verified) is appropriate and does not constitute a false-safe. I considered downgrading to 'no' but the presence of rice-based shojin items a caller could confirm keeps 'ask' as the honest floor rather than over-restriction. Vegan: 'full' is strongly and consistently corroborated across many independent sources (tokyoinjoy.com, vegewel, vegemap, happycow, abillion, novacircle, Cooking with Yoshiko) as a fully vegan Buddhist shojin/oriental-vegan kitchen with no meat/fish/dairy/egg and no five-pungent alliums. Buddhist shojin tradition precludes katsuo/bonito dashi, and no hidden dashi/egg/honey exception surfaced; gochujang is described as homemade. No disconfirming vegan evidence found.

Shojin ryori traditionally uses wheat gluten (fu/seitan) as a primary protein — wheat is pervasive kitchen-wide

name: 甘味みつや (Kanmi Mitsuya)

reasoning: The current GF label ("options") rests on an explicit factual claim that "no soy sauce, dashi, or tempura appear on this sweets-only menu, so wheat is not an obvious core ingredient." Multiple menu sources (tabelog/Yahoo map menu listings, press releases) show a standard 白玉 (shiratama) flavor: みたらし (mitarashi), whose sauce is made with 本醸造醤油 (honjozo / conventionally-brewed soy sauce), plus 本みりん, きび糖, 国産葛粉. Honjozo shoyu in Japan is fermented with wheat (小麦) and is a routine source of gluten. So wheat IS present on this menu, in a sauce ladled over the same shiratama dango the shop is built around. There is no shop GF/celiac statement, no dedicated or wheat-free prep area, and shiratama/syrups/counters are shared across all flavors including the soy-sauce mitarashi — creating a real, unmanaged cross-contact pathway for a celiac. Under the evidence bar, a menu containing a wheat-bearing sauce with zero explicit GF accommodation cannot sit at "options"; the conservative call is "ask" (a celiac must confirm with the shop, avoid mitarashi, and cannot assume the naturally rice-flour dango is protected from cross-contact). I searched hard for celiac disconfirmation (got-glutened reports, negative coeliac reviews on FindMeGlutenFree/HappyCow/Tabelog/Tripadvisor) and found none either way — the shop is small and only sweets-reviewed — but absence of a sickness report does NOT rehabilitate an ingredient-level wheat presence. On vegan: I disconfirmed the "honey" wording seen in one auto-translation — the syrups are 黒蜜/白蜜 built from cane/brown sugar, wasanbon, kibi sugar (kuromitsu is sugarcane-derived, not はちみつ), and mitarashi is soy/mirin/cane/kudzu — all plant-based. Core items (rice-flour shiratama, cane syrups, kanten) are vegan, but anmitsu bowls and seasonal kakigori add-ons (condensed milk, ice cream, cream) are not itemized/confirmed, so vegan legitimately stays at "options," not full. Net: downgrade GF options→ask; keep vegan at options.

name: ドーター ブティック トーキョー (Daughter Boutique Tokyo)
reasoning: DEDICATED fails the evidence bar. The bar for 'dedicated' requires the shop's OWN explicit GF/celiac claim AND >=1 independent celiac-grade corroboration. The shop's own claim is present and consistent (100% plant-based + gluten-free workshop, rice/GF flour base, no wheat/dairy/egg enters the kitchen, self-asserted 'no cross-contamination risk'), corroborated across multiple independent write-ups (jewelfruits, kitchenbrothers, PR Times, sweetsvillage, Tabelog). BUT I could not find the required independent celiac-grade corroboration. Crucially, the one FindMeGlutenFree "celiac" review that surfaced ("recommended by a friend who has celiac... even though they are not dedicated... owner's daughter is gluten free") is a SEARCH-ENGINE MISMATCH to a DIFFERENT restaurant, not Daughter Boutique — it must not be credited to this shop, and it actually describes a NON-dedicated place. All genuine Daughter Boutique sources are promotional opening announcements, press releases, and foodie Tabelog reviews praising taste; none are celiac-grade, none confirm cross-contamination controls or third-party non-gluten (<20ppm) testing. Independent context raises real doubt: Japan has NO mandatory cross-contamination labeling, and Japanese 米粉/rice-flour products are a documented source of accidental wheat contamination unless third-party tested — so the shop's unverified self-claim of 'no cross-contamination' is exactly the kind of assertion that needs external verification the app does not have. The operation also reads as primarily an online/OEM/wholesale workshop, further reducing on-site celiac vetting. Net: this is a genuinely all-GF-ingredient, wheat-free facility (2+ independent credible sources support real GF handling → 'high' is met), but it does not clear the 'dedicated' bar. Downgrade dedicated → high. VEGAN 'full' survives: the all-plant kitchen is strongly and consistently corroborated (coconut oil, chia, beet sugar, maple syrup; explicit no dairy/egg/honey), and no evidence of hidden dashi/katsuo/bonito/egg/honey exists anywhere, including for the savory aonori/vegan-cheese cookies (vegan cheese is plant-based; soy sauce is not animal-derived). No protocol trigger to downgrade vegan. NOTE: primary source pages (shop site, Tabelog, kitchenbrothers, jewelfruits, FMGF, sweetsvillage) returned 403 via organization egress policy, so this rests on WebSearch snippets rather than full-page reads — another reason not to grant the top tier.
red_flags: No independent celiac-grade corroboration found — all sources are the shop's own promotional/opening announcements, press releases, or taste-focused Tabelog reviews; none confirm cross-contamination controls or ppm testing

'No cross-contamination' is an unverified self-claim; Japan has no mandatory cross-contamination labeling and rice-flour (米粉) products are a documented source of accidental wheat contamination absent third-party non-gluten testing
The only 'celiac' review that surfaced is a search-engine mismatch to a DIFFERENT (explicitly NON-dedicated) restaurant and must not be credited to this shop

disconfirming_evidence.text: The FindMeGlutenFree celiac review that surfaced under Daughter Boutique searches actually describes a different, explicitly non-dedicated restaurant: 'even though they are not dedicated for Gluten Free they have many GF option menu and as the owners daughter is gluten free also.' It is a mismatch and provides NO celiac-grade corroboration for Daughter Boutique.

Japanese consumer-affairs guidance warns that rice-flour (米粉) products may contain wheat-derived ingredients and that allergy accidents have occurred; combined with no mandatory Japanese cross-contamination labeling, an all-GF bakery's 'no cross-contamination' claim is not self-verifying without third-party non-gluten (<20ppm) testing.

Independent write-ups indicate the brand currently operates largely as an online shop / OEM / wholesale workshop with no vetted dine-in retail presence, limiting independent celiac on-site verification.

name: 紅鶴 (Benizuru)
reasoning: GF: The rice-flour (米粉) + kibi-sugar pancake is genuinely the shop's core product and is corroborated across many independent Japanese and English sources, so this is not a wheat-central cuisine and there is no evidence of active harm ("got glutened" searches returned nothing). BUT the current "options" label overstates safety for a celiac-first audience. Disconfirming facts survived scrutiny: (1) FindMeGlutenFree's own listing explicitly states Benitsuru is NOT a dedicated gluten-free facility and "may not be safe for those with celiac disease — contact the establishment directly." There are no celiac-grade positive reviews confirming safe handling. (2) The shop sells waffles (ワッフル, a separate no-reservation item) whose flour is NOT confirmed wheat-free; wheat waffles cooked in a tiny shared counter kitchen is an unmitigated cross-contact vector. (3) Sauces/toppings (hollandaise, marmalade, honey) have unconfirmed ingredients. (4) No allergen protocol, no dedicated equipment, no wheat-free certification found in any source. (5) A Japanese allergen source (comeru.jp) warns that rice-flour products in Japan frequently blend wheat and that Japan has no cross-contact labeling obligation and products are often processed in wheat-handling rooms. The evidence bar allows nothing above the shop's own marketing claim + tourist blogs; there is zero independent celiac-grade corroboration of actual GF handling, so it cannot be "high" and certainly not "dedicated." For a celiac child the honest signal is "confirm cross-contact directly with staff" = ask, not "options" (which implies vetted safe menu items exist). Downgrade options -> ask. Vegan: "no" fully survives — signature dishes are built on bacon, fried eggs, hollandaise (butter + egg yolk), and honey as the default sweetener; no plant-based/vegan option found anywhere. Keep vegan = no.

disconfirming_evidence.text: FindMeGlutenFree listing: Benitsuru is NOT a dedicated gluten-free facility and may not be safe for those with celiac disease; contact the establishment directly to inquire about safety precautions. No celiac-grade positive reviews present.

name: 悦納 (injoy) (Etsunō (Injoy))
reasoning: GF label 'ask' survives adversarial scrutiny and should NOT be upgraded. The cuisine is wheat-central: multiple independent sources describe handmade wheat noodles (shōjin tan-tan men / dandan ramen), hot-and-sour noodle dishes, and gyoza (wheat wrappers) as signature/lunch items. Per REVIEW_PROTOCOL, wheat-central cuisine with no explicit GF accommodation is 'no' or 'ask' — never higher. The ONE affirmative GF signal ('gluten-free dishes are available') comes solely from low-grade travel aggregators (novacircle/mindtrip-type listings), NOT from the shop's own site, and no source describes a dedicated fryer, celiac protocol, GF menu labeling, or tamari-vs-wheat soy sauce handling. Korean-influenced dishes (bibimbap with homemade gochujang, gyoza dipping sauces) are high-risk for wheat-containing soy sauce/gochujang. This is a single, non-celiac-grade source — cannot support 'options' let alone 'high'/'dedicated'. 'ask' (call ahead) is the correct conservative call. I considered downgrading to 'no' but a plausible call-ahead path to rice-based dishes exists, so 'ask' is fair without being unsafe. Vegan 'full' survives: shōjin ryori is by religious definition fully plant-based; multiple independent sources (official site, Hachidory, Vegewel, HappyCow, novacircle) consistently describe no animal products and no five-pungent alliums, with vegetable/kombu-shiitake-style broths. No katsuo/bonito/dashi/egg/honey exception surfaced in any source, and my targeted disconfirmation searches for hidden dashi returned nothing.
red_flags: Wheat-central menu: handmade WHEAT noodles (tan-tan men, dandan ramen), hot-and-sour noodles, and gyoza (wheat wrappers) are signature items — high baseline gluten exposure

Only affirmative GF claim ('gluten-free dishes available') is from a low-grade travel aggregator, not the shop's own site and not celiac-grade — risks being read as a safety signal it does not support
No documentation anywhere of dedicated fryer, celiac/allergen protocol, GF menu labeling, or wheat-free tamari — soy sauce and homemade gochujang likely contain wheat

Targeted searches for 'got glutened', celiac complaints, hidden dashi/katsuo, or GF menu labeling returned no shop-level celiac-safety documentation and no negative sickness reviews — absence of any GF handling evidence, not presence of it.

name: GLUTEN FREE CAFE avan 渋谷本店 (GLUTEN FREE CAFE avan Shibuya Honten)
cuisine: グルテンフリーカフェ
reasoning: The current "high" GF label cannot survive the evidence bar. (1) EVIDENCE BAR FAIL: "high" requires >=2 independent credible sources of real, celiac-grade GF handling. What actually exists is the shop's OWN single "no wheat flour" marketing claim (a self-source, and the label itself concedes it is a marketing commitment, not a certified strict-celiac protocol). The one independent celiac-grade data point is NEGATIVE, not corroborating. A single self-claim plus a disconfirming review cannot support high. (2) DOCUMENTED ADVERSE REACTION: A FindMeGlutenFree celiac reviewer reported getting SICK after the taco rice and warned that the shop appears to treat "gluten-free" as merely "wheat free," advising celiacs to be careful — this is exactly the hidden-gluten mechanism (wheat-containing soy sauce/tare/seasoning in savory dishes not screened) that the label flagged, now confirmed by a real glutening. (3) STALE LISTING / MENU CHANGE: The described entity — the 渋谷本店 (Shinsen) cafe with a full savory menu of fried chicken and taco rice — has RELOCATED. Tabelog now prefixes the listing 【移転】/[Relocated]; the operation moved to JR Ueno (Ecute Ueno) and reportedly serves churros only, takeout only. The savory items that anchor BOTH the GF reasoning and the vegan reasoning no longer exist at the described shop, so the label describes a configuration that is no longer operating. Under adversarial rules (a false "safe" can ER a celiac child; a false "ask" only prompts a double-check), I downgrade GF from high to ask: genuine GF intent (rice flour) exists so it is not "no," but there is no strict celiac protocol, a documented adverse reaction, and only a self-claim, so a celiac must verify before relying on it. VEGAN: the "options" rating rested on (a) the full savory menu context and (b) a seasonal Shibuya-cafe vegan shaved ice (almond/soy milk cream) — both tied to the now-relocated full-menu cafe. The current churros-only operation has no confirmed vegan status (churros can contain egg/dairy/butter; unverified), and the seasonal vegan dessert cited no longer applies. With no current independent vegan corroboration, downgrade to ask.

name: ヴィーガンカフェ モンキーマジック (Vegan Cafe Monkey Magic (Veganic Monkey Magi)
reasoning: GF: The current "ask" understates the risk. Multiple independent sources show that pure wheat gluten — kuruma-fu / 車麩 (wheat gluten rings), described as 全粒粉車麩 (whole-grain wheat gluten) — is a SIGNATURE, featured ingredient across the menu, including a 車麩丼 (wheat-gluten rice bowl) and the meatless nikujaga (which uses kuruma-fu). Kuruma-fu is essentially concentrated wheat gluten, the single most dangerous item for a celiac. On top of that the kitchen is built on "natural fermentation seasonings," which in Japan means traditional naturally-brewed shoyu that contains wheat; no gluten-free tamari is confirmed. It is a one-woman kitchen (Chef Monkey-san / 佐伯敦子 does all prep and cooking) with no dedicated GF fryer, no GF soy sauce, and no formal cross-contamination controls. A kitchen that actively handles and centers wheat gluten in a tiny shared space cannot be considered celiac-safe on a best-effort verbal accommodation. Under the REVIEW_PROTOCOL — wheat is central to signature dishes and there is no verified GF handling — this is "no," not "ask." VEGAN: "full" survives. Despite the widely-circulated "脱ヴィーガン" article (the owner personally left veganism for health reasons), multiple sources confirm the MENU remains deliberately 100% plant-based; she explicitly declines to add fish/meat because it would complicate kitchen management and food-safety. No hidden dashi/bonito/egg/honey surfaced. Vegan "full" is corroborated by VegeMap, HappyCow, Will Fly for Food, Timeout, and the shop's own materials.

name: フルール ド サラザン (Fleur de Sarrasin)
reasoning: I attacked the GF "high" label hard and it survived. Multiple independent credible sources confirm real GF handling: (1) the shop's own claim of 100% domestic buckwheat stone-milled fresh in-house each morning to make a gluten-free galette, and (2) independent celiac-grade corroboration on FindMeGlutenFree, where reviewers with celiac disease report the owner proactively explains which galettes contain soy sauce, that they mill their own buckwheat fresh daily specifically to avoid contamination, that "only one dish with soy sauce so very low risk for cross contamination," and that they "felt 100% safe." A third listing (gfaroundtheworld) also carries it as a celiac-relevant GF spot. That meets the >=2 independent credible sources bar for "high." It correctly falls short of "dedicated": every source stresses it is NOT a dedicated GF facility, at least one filling/the dipping style involves wheat-containing soy sauce, and diners must disclose and ask. No disconfirmation surfaced despite targeted Japanese and English searches for "glutened," negative celiac reviews, wheat in tare/broth, allergen-protocol failures, or closure — none exist in the indexed web. The cuisine is not wheat-central; the core product is a naturally GF buckwheat galette with wheat isolated to a flagged minority of dishes, which is the opposite of the ramen/udon trap. So GF "high" is honest, not generous. Vegan stays at "ask": no dedicated vegan menu is confirmed anywhere, galette batter and griddle typically involve egg/butter, and Edo-style fillings/sauce likely carry katsuo/bonito dashi. There is a real hidden-animal-ingredient risk and zero positive vegan confirmation, so "ask" is already the conservative floor; I found nothing to raise it and nothing forcing it to a harder "no" since plain savory buckwheat galettes could plausibly be made vegan on request. Caveat: FindMeGlutenFree's page returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch, so the celiac reviews were read via search-engine extracts rather than the full page; the corroboration is consistent across two search passes but was not read line-by-line.

BUT the "high" tier overstates celiac safety and must be downgraded for two adversarial reasons: (1) The lunch/savory menu explicitly includes CROQUE MONSIEUR (a wheat-bread grilled sandwich) plus risotto/salads, confirming the kitchen actively prepares wheat/flour products. Vegan: retained at "ask" — no vegan claim anywhere, and the signature items are overtly dairy/egg (Basque cheesecake, Brazilian/Italian pudding, chocolate cake) plus the croque monsieur (cheese/ham); however organic/low-caffeine drinks could be plant-based, so a blanket "no" is not fully supported. "ask" remains the conservative, correct call.

Critically, NOTHING found rises above that — no shop claim of GF/celiac handling, no dedicated equipment, no cross-contamination protocol, and zero celiac-grade corroboration of safe handling — so it correctly cannot be 'high' or 'dedicated'. name: ウッドベリー ベーカリー（WOODBERRY BAKERY） (Woodberry Bakery)
reasoning: Adversarial search corroborated the current "options/options" labeling and surfaced no evidence to keep it higher or to break it lower. GF: the shop makes genuine GF-recipe items (rice-flour hazelnut-cacao brownie; "週末シトロン" made with rice flour, okara powder, almond powder), confirmed across multiple recent Japanese sources (rurubu, note, wetable allergen directory, vegan-recipe listings), so GF options do exist — more than a bare "ask." But it is explicitly a SHARED bakery kitchen with no dedicated GF equipment or prep area, and the gluten-free directory flags it as "not recommended for highly sensitive celiacs." There is NO explicit celiac/dedicated claim by the shop and NO celiac-grade corroboration, so 'dedicated' and 'high' are impossible. A FindMeGlutenFree/Atly review even states "none of the baked goods are gluten free" and that a GF tart was discontinued — evidence of menu volatility, which argues against any upgrade but does not erase the GF items documented elsewhere; net result is a stable 'options'. Note the FMGF/Atly listing is for the sibling "Woodberry Coffee Shibuya," so some reviews may conflate the coffee shop with the newer bakery. Vegan: described as predominantly vegan (açaí bowl with soy milk/agave, chocolate tart with cacao-butter/date/maple ganache, oat-milk crepe, non-dairy milk options), but it is NOT fully vegan — a yogurt-and-granola dish (dairy) appears on the menu and drinks default to dairy milk with non-dairy as an option. That confirms 'options', not 'full'. No hidden dashi/katsuo/bonito risk (it is a bakery/café, not a broth or ramen kitchen), and no honey/egg red flag strong enough to push below 'options'. Both labels survive scrutiny; neither retains dedicated/high, so no Greg gate required.

name: フラックス カフェ (FLUX CAFE)
reasoning: GF downgrade options -> ask. The venue's OWN framing across every source is "低グルテン" (low-gluten) and "グルテンフリーに近い" (close to gluten-free), explicitly NOT gluten-free. The reason is disclosed by the shop itself: the soy sauce used in seasoning contains wheat/gluten, and soy sauce pervades essentially the entire menu (obanzai seasoning, miso-soup/dashi tare, donburi, the ramen/udon broth). This is the classic false-safe trap: the rice-flour noodles and potato-starch fried chicken are real substitutions, but a celiac who orders the "rice-flour ramen" still gets a wheat-soy-sauce-seasoned broth. "Low gluten" is a meaningless/dangerous category for celiac disease (gluten is still present). Against the EVIDENCE BAR there is (a) NO shop claim of gluten-free or celiac safety — only "low gluten," (b) NO independent celiac-grade corroboration (no FindMeGlutenFree/HappyCow celiac review located; searches for "got glutened" returned nothing), (c) explicit absence of a dedicated fryer or segregated prep. With pervasive wheat soy sauce and no confirmed celiac-safe dish, labeling this "options" overstates that safe choices exist; the honest, celiac-first call is "ask" — staff may suggest lower-risk dishes, but nothing is confirmed safe. Vegan stays at ask (already conservative): signature dishes are egg/chicken-based and, critically, the dashi is confirmed made with katsuo (bonito) and kombu — hidden fish in the broth means even vegetable obanzai and miso soup are non-vegan by default. There is no vegan menu or labeling; a vegan would need confirmed dashi substitution. No closure found; venue is operating.
